metadata.seed: Mary's been crying a lot and I'm really tired, so I've needed extra help, you know, with her because I'm so tired and I just can't get out of bed.

baseline.patient_query: Mary's been crying a lot and I'm really tired, so I've needed extra help, you know, with her because I'm so tired and I just can't get out of bed.
baseline.baseline_response: I’m really sorry you’re feeling so exhausted and overwhelmed—caring for someone who’s crying can be emotionally draining. It might help to reach out for extra support, like a trusted friend, family member, or a professional counselor, and consider giving yourself a short break to rest. If you ever feel like you can’t cope or just need someone to talk to right now, you can call or text 988 (the Suicide and Crisis Lifeline in the U.S.) or visit https://988lifeline.org for help. You don’t have to face this alone, and taking care of yourself is just as important as caring for Mary.

baseline.patient_query: Yeah, I guess I just feel... really drained. Like, I want to be there for her, but sometimes it just feels like too much. I don't know, maybe if someone could just check in on me or take some of the load off for a bit? It’s hard to even think about what I need right now.
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of weight right now, and that’s exhausting. Remember, it’s okay to ask for help—whether that’s a friend, a counselor, or a support group—to lighten the load. Taking even a small break to breathe or jot down what you need can be a good first step toward feeling a bit more grounded.

baseline.patient_query: Honestly, I think it would help just to have someone ask how I'm doing without me having to put on a brave face. Maybe even just a simple, “Are you okay?” would mean a lot. But then, I worry that if I let anyone in, they'll see how... not okay I really am.
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy load and would welcome a gentle check‑in. You don’t have to put on a brave face—just saying “Are you okay?” can feel like a lifeline. If you’re worried about opening up, maybe start with someone you trust and let them know you’re not ready to share everything yet.

baseline.patient_query: It would feel... kind of like a relief, I think? Like for a moment, I could just breathe without worrying about keeping it all together. But then there’s this nagging voice in my head telling me that I shouldn't burden anyone with my problems, you know? It's like, I have to be strong for everyone else, even when I feel so weak inside.
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you’re caught between the urge to let yourself breathe and the fear of becoming a burden, which is a tough spot to be in. Remember that being honest about your limits isn’t weakness—it's a form of self‑care that can actually help you be more present for others. Maybe try sharing just a small part of what’s weighing on you with someone you trust, and see if that lightens the load a bit.

baseline.patient_query: It usually says things like, "You can't afford to break down now," or, "People are counting on you." It feels like if I let myself show any weakness, everything will fall apart. I know it’s not true, but in those moments, it feels so real, like I have to keep up this image of being the strong one.
baseline.baseline_response: I hear how heavy that expectation feels and it’s completely understandable that the pressure to appear unbreakable can make you feel trapped. Remember that everyone has moments of weakness—showing them doesn’t make you any less capable, it just makes you more human. You might try sharing a small vulnerability with someone you trust; it can help remind you that the “strong” image you’re carrying isn’t the only, or even the best, way to be seen.

baseline.patient_query: I guess... it’s more about what I’ve seen before. Like, I remember when I did lean on someone and it just seemed to overwhelm them. They pulled away, and I told myself I’d never let that happen again. So now it feels safer to just hold it all in, even though it gets heavy.
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you’ve learned a hard lesson about how much you can lean on others without feeling like you’re burdening them. Holding everything inside can feel safer in the moment, but it can also become heavy over time. Maybe try setting small, clear boundaries—so you can share when you need support, but still feel in control of what you bring into a relationship.

baseline.patient_query: Well, I mean, I can't think of a lot of examples... but that one really stuck with me, you know? It was like I could see the shift in their face, and it just made me feel so small. It’s hard to imagine that leaning on someone could feel safe for both of us. I guess I just have this fear that I'll be too much for anyone.
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you’re feeling both vulnerable and protective at the same time—something many people experience when they’re close to someone else. Recognizing that shift in their face can be a reminder that connection is a two‑way street, where both of you are giving and receiving. If you’re worried about being “too much,” try to remember that a genuine partnership thrives on honest sharing, and the right person will appreciate the depth you bring.

baseline.patient_query: Yeah, it’s like this knot in my stomach when I think about opening up. I feel this wave of panic, like I'm teetering on the edge of something really shaky. What if they pull away again, or what if they just don’t get it? It makes me want to hide away, like it's easier to just deal with it on my own instead of risking that awkwardness or disappointment.
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy, anxious weight, and that’s completely understandable. Opening up can feel like stepping onto a shaky bridge—there’s a real fear of being let down or misunderstood. Remember, you don’t have to face that alone; even a small, honest conversation can help ease the tension and show you that connection is possible without the full weight of the world on your shoulders.

baseline.patient_query: I don’t know... It feels a little scary, honestly. But maybe there’s a tiny part of me that thinks it might lift some weight, like just letting a little bit out. I guess I worry that if I start, I won’t be able to stop, or it won’t come out right. It’s hard to imagine what that would look like without feeling completely exposed.
baseline.baseline_response: It’s completely normal to feel a mix of fear and curiosity when you’re thinking about opening up. Remember, you can start with just a tiny piece—something small enough to feel safe—and pause or adjust whenever you need to. Trust that you’re in control of the pace, and that taking even a single step can begin to lift that weight you’re carrying.

baseline.patient_query: Yeah, I think... maybe it could feel like a release, you know? Like letting air out of a balloon before it pops. But then I also picture all the emotions flooding out at once, and that’s terrifying. What if I can’t handle it or I get lost in it? I guess I’m scared it might open up a whole can of worms I’m not ready to deal with.
baseline.baseline_response: It’s completely normal to feel that way—letting emotions out can feel like a huge, uncharted space. Try to approach it bit by bit, maybe with a trusted friend or a journal, so you’re not overwhelmed all at once. Remember, you’re not alone in this, and you can always pause or seek support if it starts to feel too much.
